coredata.dc:title: Ultra-fine carbon nanosheets from coal oxidation for tri-functional improvement of carbon nanofiber fabrics
coredata.dc:creator: Gan, Xuemeng
Yuan, Renlu
Zhu, Jiayao
Li, Qiqi
Tang, Tingting
Qin, Fuwei
Zhu, Linna
Zhang, Jun
Wang, Luxiang
Zhang, Su
Song, Huaihe
Jia, Dianzeng
coredata.dc:description: The development of advanced coal-based functional nanomaterials is of great significance for clean and effective utilization of coal resources. Herein, we report novel coal-based ultra-fine carbon nanosheets (UCNSs) prepared by chemical oxidation of bituminous coal with a high yield of 40.0 wt%. UCNSs show a lamellar size of 10–20 nm, mainly composed of 63.5 at.% carbon, 5.1 at.% nitrogen, and 31.4 at.% oxygen. The structural merits of suitable size distribution, enriched functional groups, and highly conjugated core enable the UCNSs showing a tri-functional reinforcement for carbon nanofibers prepared by electrospinning and carbonization. Thanks to the strong cross-linking effect and formation of entire conductive networks, the UCNS-embedded carbon nanofiber fabrics show 32.6 and 1.5 times improved mechanical strength and electrical conductivity, respectively, than the one without UCNSs. What's more, the exposed edges of UCNSs act as active sites for ultrafast capacitive energy storage, leading to the free-standing fabric with significantly improved capacitance and rate performance (191.2 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, 113.5 F g−1 at 50 A g−1) for aqueous supercapacitors. This work may provide a new thought for high-efficient and high-value-added utilization of coal resources.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanosheets
Carbon nanofibers
Supercapacitors
Coal
Oxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the reinforced carbon nanofiber fabrics (RCNFs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation and separation of GQDs and UCNSs, preparation of UCNS reinforced carbon nanofiber fabrics (RCNFs) through electrospinning and carbonization. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a, b) HRTEM images of UCNSs, (c) XPS survey spectra, (d) FT-IR spectra, (e) XRD patterns, and (f) Raman spectra of UCNSs and bituminous coal. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photographs of (a1-a3) CNF without adding UCNSs and (b1-b4) RCNF-0.3 before and after bending and folding. (c) Photographs of RCNF-0.3 (0.85 mm, 300 mg) pulls up 150 g weights. (d) The mechanical strength of RCNF-x. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XRD patterns, (b) the ratios of intensity to FWHM of the (002) peaks, (c) Raman spectra, (d) electric conductivity of the CNF and RCNF-x. (e) XPS survey spectra, and (f) XPS C1s spectra of the CNF and RCNF-0.3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) CV curves at 10 mV s−1, (b) rate performance, (c) Nyquist plots, (d) the calculated charge-transfer resistances (Rct) of the CNF and RCNF-x. (e) CV curves at 10 mV s−1 of RCNF-0.3 and G-CNF. Schematic illustration of the pseudocapacitive energy storage behavior of the (f1) RCNF-0.3 and (f2) G-CNF. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) CV curves at various scan rates, (b) Ragone plots, and (c) cycling stability at 10 A g−1 of the RCNF-0.3//RCNF-0.3 supercapacitor. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Ultra-fine carbon nanosheets from coal oxidation for tri-functional improvement of carbon nanofiber fabrics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

School of Materials Science and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors with equal contribution to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of advanced coal-based functional nanomaterials is of great significance for clean and effective utilization of coal resources. Herein, we report novel coal-based ultra-fine carbon nanosheets (UCNSs) prepared by chemical oxidation of bituminous coal with a high yield of 40.0 wt%. UCNSs show a lamellar size of 10–20 nm, mainly composed of 63.5 at.% carbon, 5.1 at.% nitrogen, and 31.4 at.% oxygen. The structural merits of suitable size distribution, enriched functional groups, and highly conjugated core enable the UCNSs showing a tri-functional reinforcement for carbon nanofibers prepared by electrospinning and carbonization. Thanks to the strong cross-linking effect and formation of entire conductive networks, the UCNS-embedded carbon nanofiber fabrics show 32.6 and 1.5 times improved mechanical strength and electrical conductivity, respectively, than the one without UCNSs. What's more, the exposed edges of UCNSs act as active sites for ultrafast capacitive energy storage, leading to the free-standing fabric with significantly improved capacitance and rate performance (191.2 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, 113.5 F g−1 at 50 A g−1) for aqueous supercapacitors. This work may provide a new thought for high-efficient and high-value-added utilization of coal resources.

Ultra-fine carbon nanosheets with a high yield of 40.0 wt% were prepared through liquid-phase oxidation of bituminous coal, showing tri-functional (mechanical/electrical/electrochemical) reinforcement for carbon nanofiber fabrics.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Coal has been widely used as fossil fuel or raw materials for chemical industry due to its natural abundance, high carbon content, and low cost [1–5]. Developing advanced coal-based carbon materials is one of the most promising way to overcome the serious environmental pollution, low value-addition, and coal resources waste in traditional coal utilization [6–11]. Up to now, various advanced carbon materials such as coal-based graphene [12–16], carbon nanotubes [17–20], and porous carbon membrane [21–23] are prepared from coal through, for example, high temperature treatment, arc-discharge, and/or chemical oxidation methods. These materials show unique morphological, electrical, and mechanical properties for high-end applications [24–26] such as electronic devices [27,28], supercapacitors [29–33], and solar cells [34]. However, complex and harsh preparation conditions are usually required for preparing coal-based nanomaterials due to the highly cross-linked molecular structure of coal with high chemical inertness, inhibiting their large-scale and controlled preparation.

Liquid-phase oxidation has been demonstrated as an effective route to prepare advanced coal-based nanomaterials. Typically, Tour [35,36] and Qiu [37] presented the preparation of coal-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by oxidation cutting the conjugated macromolecular structure of coal. These GQDs possess unique properties such as high chemical reactivity [38–40], tunable bandgaps [41], and novel photoluminescence [42], which have been applied for optical devices [43,44], ion detection [37], electrocatalysis [45,46], energy storage [47], and sensors [48]. Our recent works have showed that the coal-based GQDs could be used as novel building blocks for constructing advanced carbon materials through bottom-up strategies [49–51]. Thanks to the high chemical reactivity, they could cross-link with polyacrylonitrile precursor through edge-functional groups and construct entire reinforcing phase and conductive network in the electrospun carbon nanofibers, leading to the improved mechanical strength and conductivity [52–54]. However, limited by the low yield (generally less than 10 wt% from the feed coal) and complex preparation procedure, GQDs have only been explored in fundamental research till now. Apart from GQDs, Chi et al. have observed the existence of graphene oxide, carbon dots, and graphite microcrystals in the coal oxidation products [55], revealing the preparation of other functional nanocarbons by liquid-phase oxidation of coal.

Herein, we report the preparation of ultra-fine carbon nanosheets (UCNSs) with a high yield of 40.0 wt% through liquid-phase oxidation of bituminous coal and centrifugal classification. The UCNSs show highly conjugated core, unique size distribution of 10–20 nm, abundant edge sites, and enriched functional groups (5.1 at.% nitrogen and 31.4 at.% oxygen). As an exemplification for application, UCNSs are added to the spinning solution to prepare reinforced carbon nanofiber fabrics (RCNFs). The embedded UCNSs construct entire reinforcing phase and conductive networks inside the carbon nanofibers, so greatly improve the mechanical strength and electrical conductivity of 32.6 and 1.5 times, respectively. Moreover, the suitable size of UCNSs enables their partially exposed edges on the surface of carbon nanofibers, providing abundant oxygen-bearing functional groups as pseudocapacitive sites. Therefore, the RCNFs show significantly improved capacitance as free-standing electrodes for supercapacitors.

The bituminous coal is purchased from Xinjiang Heishan Coal chemical Co. LTD, China, then milled and passed through 200 mesh sieve to obtained the coal power with the particle size less than 75 μm. UCNSs were prepared by acid oxidation of the bituminous coal powder and subsequent centrifugal classification. Typically, 30 g coal powder, 250 mL HNO3 (67 wt%), and 750 mL H2SO4 (98 wt%) were slowly added into a 5 L jacketed glass reactor with stirring, temperature control, and reflux functions. It was reacted at 65 °C heated by a circulating water system for 10 h with mild stirring, then naturally cooled to room temperature. After careful dilution using 5 L deionized water and dialyzed to neutral (dialysis bag molecular weight cut off is 3500 Da), the product was first centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min to separate coal-based GQDs (supernatant) and the precipitate (Fig. 1 ). The precipitate was re-dispersed into 20 L deionized water. Then, the dispersion was centrifuged again at 4000 rpm for 1 min to remove the inadequately-oxidized large particles. The UCNSs was obtained by drying the supernatant at 80 °C and milling to fine powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the reinforced carbon nanofiber fabrics (RCNFs)
ce:section.ce:para: 0.4 g polyacrylonitrile powder (PAN, J&K Scientific Co. Ltd., average molecular weight: 150,000 Da) and certain amount (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4 g) of the UCNSs were completely dispersed into 5 mL N, N-dimethylformamide (AR, Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute) at 80 °C by vigorous stirring and sonication. The solution was then added in a plastic syringe with a stainless steel needle (inner diameter: 1.1 mm) for electrospinning to prepare UCNS reinforced PAN-based nanofibers. The applied voltage of electrospinning is 20 kV. The temperature and humidity were controlled to be 40 °C and 40%, respectively.

The RCNF-x (x is the mass of UCNSs) were prepared by direct carbonizing the UCNS reinforced PAN-based nanofibers at 800 °C for 1 h in a N2 atmosphere with the heating rate of 2 °C min−1. For comparison, we have prepared carbon nanofiber fabric without adding UCNSs (CNF), and coal-based GQD reinforced carbon nanofiber fabric (G-CNF) by the similar route but adding 0.3 g GQDs instead of UCNSs. The Stabilized-CNF and Stabilized-RCNF-0.3 were also prepared with an additional pre-oxidation step in air at 280 °C for 2 h before carbonization [52,53].

The structure and composition of the samples were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Japan), high-resolution transmission microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2100F, Japan), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker VERTEX 70, Germany), X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Germany), Raman spectroscopy (Bruker SENTERRA, Germany), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 Xi), 13C Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (13C NMR, Bruker 400WB AVANCE III).

The samples were cut into the square sheets (1.0 × 1.0 cm2, ca. 2.0 mg, 0.09 mm thick) and pressed between two nickel foams as the working electrodes in three-electrode system, while directly used as the free-standing working electrodes (0.7 cm2, ca.1.0 mg, 0.09 mm thick) in symmetric supercapacitors. The assembly of three-electrode and two-electrode systems in 6 mol L−1 KOH electrolyte is according to our previous works [52,56]. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., China).

As illustrated in Fig. 1, the general model of bituminous coal is considered as the polycyclic aromatic units cross-linked by aliphatic chains [57]. Through chemical oxidation, the aliphatic chains are primarily oxidized and cut because of the relatively high chemical activity, leaving behind the polycyclic aromatic units decorated with abundant oxygen-bearing functional groups at the edge sites [58]. Thereafter, GQDs and UCNSs are separated by purification and centrifugal classification. UCNSs have a higher product yield of ca. 40.0 wt% (ca. 12 g from 30 g raw coal from one-pot) than ca. 10.0 wt% of GQDs, which is beneficial for large-scale production and practical application. From the HRTEM images (Fig. 2 a), large numbers of UCNSs “debris” are observed with the lamellar diameters ranging from 10 to 20 nm, which are much larger than the GQDs (diameter: 2–4 nm) [53,59]. No clear lattice fingerprint is observed from the zoomed image in Fig. 2b, indicating their relatively low crystallinity. When designing the experimental scheme, we have also considered and tried other types of coals as the precursors including anthracitic and brown coals. The anthracitic coal shows high chemical inertness due to its high coalification degree and highly conjugated structure, leading to low yields of GQDs and UCNSs even after long-term chemical oxidation. Conversely, brown coal has very poor crystallinity and extremely high reactivity, resulting in very low yield. Therefore, the bituminous coal has suitable conjugated structure and moderate chemical activity is suggested as an optimal choice.

The XPS survey spectrum shows that bituminous coal (simply marked as coal) with a moderate coalification degree contains 6.7 at.% oxygen and 0.6 at.% nitrogen (Fig. 2c). Correspondingly, the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows that coal mainly has –CH3/–CH2– (ca. 2900 cm−1), –CC (1600 cm−1), and small amount of C–O–C (1200 cm−1) groups. After oxidation by HNO3/H2SO4 mixed acid, the oxygen and nitrogen contents of UCNSs are sharply increased to 31.4 at.% and 5.1 at.%, respectively. The obvious peaks located at ca. 3400, 1730, and 1261 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of UCNSs are indexed as –COOH/-OH, –CO, and C–O–C, respectively. The strong absorption peak located at ca. 1350 cm−1 demonstrates the presence of –NO2 due to using HNO3 as oxidant [52]. The result is in well accordance with the XPS C1s and 13C NMR spectrum results given in Figs. S1 and S2, and Table S1.

The coal contains some residual minerals such as CaCO3, CaMg(CO3)2, as revealed from the sharp XRD peaks located at ca. 30° (Fig. S3). The ash component is removed after chemical oxidation and multi-step purification. The UCNSs show only one broad and dispersive diffraction peak located at ca. 22° (Fig. 2e). Looking back to the preparation of graphite oxide by chemical oxidation, decoration of oxygen-bearing functional groups on the basal plane of graphite leads to an obvious expansion of the interlayer spacing from 0.335 nm to more than 0.7 nm due to the pillar effect of functional groups [60,61]. Thus, the characteristic (002) diffraction peak is shifted from 26° to ca. 11° depending on the oxidation degree. Herein, the almost remained peak position at 22° of UCNSs reveals that the abundant oxygen-bearing functional groups are mainly decorated at their edge sites due to their small sized structure (Fig. S4), which is similar to the GQDs and the carbon dots prepared by our previous works [56,62]. The peaks located at ca. 1360 and 1580 cm−1 of the Raman spectra are the D- and G-bands of carbon, respectively. The AD/AG ratio calculated based on the peak area (Fig. 2f) decreases from 2.97 to 1.98 after oxidation, indicating that UCNSs show an increased conjugation degree than coal owing to removal of the highly-reactive defects and aliphatic chains, which is further confirmed by the intensified –CC peak in the FT-IR spectra. Therefore, the structure of UCNSs contains a highly conjugated core and abundant functional groups at the edge sites.

In the previous works, we have revealed that coal-based GQDs could improve the mechanical strength and electrical conductivity of carbon nanofibers because of the strong cross-linking effect and highly conjugated structure [52,53]. To illustrate the novel property and application of UCNSs, we have also prepared the UCNS reinforced carbon nanofiber fabrics (RCNFs) through electrospinning and direct carbonization, showing a more interesting tri-functional reinforcement effect. It is worth mentioning that the carbon yield increase from 42.1 wt% of CNF to 59.8 wt% of RCNF-0.3 due to the high thermal stability of the highly conjugated UCNSs, as well as the strong cross-linking effect (Table S2). The SEM images of the RCNF-x (Fig. S5) show the increased diameters of the nanofibers from 40 ± 14 nm of CNF to 240 ± 20 nm of RCNF-0.3, and 300 ± 17 nm of RCNF-0.4 due to the increased viscosity of the spinning solutions [63,64]. The carbon nanofiber fabric without adding UCNSs (CNF) shows weak and fragile feature. It can only be pick up gently by tweezers. A slight folding breaks it into pieces (Fig. 3 a1-a3). Apparently, it is not suitable to be used as the free-standing electrodes. By contrast, RCNF-x all show remarkably improved flexibility and mechanical strength. It can be restored to its initial shape after repeated bending, twisting, rolling, and folding (Fig. 3b1-b4, Fig. S6). Moreover, RCNF-0.3 with a thickness of only 0.85 mm (300 mg) can pull up 150 g weights for 3 days without obvious mechanical failure (Fig. 3c, Fig. S7). The tensile test shows that the mechanical strength of the fabrics continually improved from 0.71 MPa of CNF to 23.46 MPa of RCNF-0.3, and further 43.42 MPa of RCNF-0.4 (Fig. 3d), demonstrating the formation of inner reinforcing phase by UCNSs. The mechanism is due to the strong cross-linking effect [52]. During carbonization, the oxygen-bearing functional groups at the edges of UCNSs are reacted with polyacrylonitrile chains (Fig. S8), leading to the formation of dense and strong carbon scaffold inside the carbon nanofibers as well as the improved mechanical property [52].

When PAN was used for preparing carbon fibers, stabilization process (usually pre-oxidation) was usually necessary for making a stable cross-linked structure before carbonization. Herein, the CNF and RCNFs are prepared by direct carbonization of the precursors. We have also prepared the Stabilized-CNFs with a pre-oxidation step in air at 280 °C for 2 h before carbonization for comparison (Fig. S9). The tensile strength of Stabilized-CNF without UCNSs (1.20 MPa) is slightly higher than that of CNF, but almost 20 times lower than those of the RCNFs, demonstrating that the strong cross-linking by UCNSs is more effective than pre-oxidation for stabilizing the PAN scaffold during carbonization.

Apart from contributing the entire reinforcing phase, the inlaid UCNSs also construct inner conductive networks in RCNFs thanks to their highly conjugated core as shown in the HRTEM images (Fig. S10). CNF shows a typical amorphous structure, while RCNF-0.3 is enriched with highly crystallized domains, corresponding to the crystal face of the uniformly distributed UCNSs. The XRD patterns of CNF and RCNF-x (Fig. 4 a) exhibit the characteristic (002) diffraction peaks of carbon. The crystal degree can be estimated by the ratio of intensity and full width at half maximum (FWHM, Fig. 4b). The ratios increase from 21.5 of CNF to 28.3 of RCNF-0.3 and 44.6 of RCNF-0.4, indicating the enhanced crystallinity owing to the highly conjugated structure of UCNSs. Correspondingly, the defect density estimated by the AD/AG values from Raman spectra decreases from 2.23 of CNF to 1.96 of RCNF-0.3, and 1.92 of RCNF-0.4, coming close to 1.65 of the sample UCNSs-800 obtained by directly heat-treat UCNSs at 800 °C (Fig. 4c, Fig. S11). Therefore, the electric conductivity is monotonically increase from 24.53 S m−1 of CNF to 30.88 S m−1 of RCNF-0.3, and 39.53 S m−1 of RCNF-0.4 (Fig. 4d).

As a tri-functional reinforcing phase, UCNSs also provide additional active sites for improving the capacitive energy storage ability of the RCNFs. For clear understanding, XPS is first performed to get more information about the surface properties of CNF and RCNF-x (Fig. 4e, Fig. S12). Pure polyacrylonitrile derived CNF shows a very low oxygen content of 3.4 at.%. Obviously, the surface oxygen content is increased to 12.6 at.% of RCNF-0.3 and 19.3 at.% of RCNF-0.4, indicating that the oxygen-bearing functional groups are originated from the exposed edge sites of the inlaid UCNSs [54]. The nitrogen content shows a complex variation maybe due to the simultaneous evolution of polyacrylonitrile and UCNSs derived nitrogen-bearing groups. The C1s spectra are investigated in detail as shown in Fig. 4f and Table S3. The –CO first increased from 4.6 at.% of CNF to 5.3 at.% of RCNF-0.1, and 8.3 at.% of RCNF-0.3. The –CO components endow the UCNSs possess high electrochemical activity for pseudocapacitive energy storage in aqueous electrolyte [65–67].

Some works proposed that heteroatom doping could improve the conductivity of carbon materials by changing the electronic structure [68,69]. Some other works considered that introducing N/O bearing functional groups could break the conjugated structure of carbon, resulting in a decreased electrical conductivity [70,71]. Confusion and argument are still existed till now. In this work, the improved conductivity of RCNFs is not associated with the high nitrogen and oxygen content. The as-prepared UCNSs have highly conjugated cores and abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-bearing functional groups located at the edge sites. During carbonization, the conjugated cores are easily transformed to highly conductive networks inside the carbon nanofibers, leading to the improved electric conductivity [51,52]. On the other hands, the exposed edge sites of UCNSs provide abundant surface functional groups on the carbon nanofibers due to the high thermal stability [54]. Therefore, their electric conductivity increases along with the increased UCNS dosage, which is crucial for electrochemical process.

The capacitive performance of the fabrics is first measured in a three-electrode system. All the samples show distorted CV curves with pronounced redox peaks between −0.3 and −0.8 V vs. Hg/HgO reference, indicating their combined electric double layer and pseudocapacitive behaviors (Fig. 5 a and Fig. S13). The capacitance of CNF at 1 A g−1 is 128.5 F g−1. It is greatly improved by adding UCNSs, as indicated by the enlarged enclosed CV curve areas and prolonged discharge time of the GCD profiles. Considering the relative low surface areas (20–150 m2 g−1, Fig. S14) and slightly changed nitrogen contents (ca. 2.1 at.%) of RCNF-x compared with CNF, the capacitances of the samples are mainly pseudocapacitance contributed from oxygen-bearing functional groups at the edge sites of UCNSs [72–75]. The result can be confirmed by that RCNF-0.3 show almost no capacitance in Et4NBF4/PC electrolyte from the CV curves (Fig. S15) because pseudocapacitive behavior is barely occurred in organic electrolyte [76]. As another evidence, the PAN derived carbon nanofibers tend to form a skin-core structure through pre-oxidation and carbonization [54], resulting in some covered edge sites of the inlaid UCNSs by the dense skin, as well as the decreased capacitive energy storage ability of Stabilized-RCNF-0.3 (Fig. S16).

RCNF-0.3 shows the highest –CO content, leading to the highest capacitance of 191.2, 169.8, 147.1 F g−1 at 1, 5, 20 A g−1, respectively, among all the samples. Moreover, RCNF-0.3 exhibits outstanding rate performance with a much higher capacitance retention of 59.4% at 50 A g−1 than CNF (18.7%, Fig. 5b), indicating that the inner conductive network and edge-exposed functional groups of UCNSs ensure robust electron and ion transfer processes. As further evaluated by the EIS analysis (Fig. 5c), the semicircle diameters in high frequency region are gradually decrease from RCNF-0.1 to RCNF-0.3, leading to the decreased charge-transfer resistance (Rct) from 0.19 Ω of RCNF-0.1, and 0.12 Ω of RCNF-0.3 (Fig. 5d). The reason is mainly due to the improved electric conductivity and fast ion transport enabled by developed porous structure (Fig. S14). However, excessive UCNS dosage makes enlarged fiber diameter and decreased mesopore volume, resulting in a prolonged ion transport path, restricted migration kinetics, as well as decreased capacitance and rate performance for RCNF-0.4.

To further reveal the uniqueness of the as-prepared UCNSs, we also compared the electrochemical performance of RCNF-0.3 and G-CNF using GQDs as the reinforcing phase. The RCNF-0.3 shows a much higher CV enclosed area than G-CNF (Figs. 5e and S17) and better energy storage capability. The mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 5f, the inlaid UCNSs with suitable lamellar size (10–20 nm) can easily expose their active edges on the surface of RCNF-x after a direct carbonization (Fig. 5f1). By contrast, the small-sized GQDs (2–4 nm) are mostly encapsulated and buried inside the G-CNF, providing insufficient exposed edges (Fig. 5f2). Therefore, to expose the active edges of GQDs, we have to peel off the skin of the GQD reinforced carbon nanofiber fabric by hydrothermal hydrolysis [54]. We have also tried to use graphene oxide with much larger lamellar size of several micrometers for reinforcing the carbon nanofibers. Unfortunately, graphene oxide shows rather poor compatibility to polyacrylonitrile substrate. Electrospinning cannot be done even with an extremely low adding content below 2 wt% (0.008 g for this work), as also mentioned in the previous reports [77,78]. Therefore, we emphasize that the UCNSs with suitable size distribution, enriched functional groups, and conjugated core structure enable the novel tri-functional reinforcing effect, i.e., enhancing the mechanical strength, electrical conductivity, and capacitive performance, for the electrospinning carbon nanofiber fabrics.

Benefiting from the tri-functional reinforcement, RCNF-0.3 can be directly used as the free-standing electrode without using conductive agents and binder for practical supercapacitors. The CV curves of the RCNF-0.3//RCNF-0.3 symmetric supercapacitor show quasi-rectangular shape even at an ultrahigh scan rate of 500 mV s−1, indicating the robust electrochemical kinetics and good rate performance (Fig. 6 a). It exhibits high energy densities of 5.3 and 2.2 Wh kg−1 at power densities of 249.8 W kg−1 and 23932.4 W kg−1, respectively (Fig. 6b). Moreover, an outstanding cycling stability with no obvious capacitance fading is observed after 50,000 cycles at 10 A g−1 (Fig. 6c), which is confirmed by the nearly overlapped CV curves (Fig. S18). The morphology of RCNF-0.3 is well maintained without pulverization (Fig. S19), revealing its good structural and electrochemical stability.

In summary, the ultra-fine carbon nanosheets (UCNSs) with a high yield of 40.0 wt.% is prepared by a simply liquid-phase oxidation method and centrifugal classification using bituminous coal as the precursor. UCNSs are composed of 63.5 at.% carbon, 5.1 at.% nitrogen, and 31.4 at.% oxygen, showing the lamellar size of 10–20 nm, highly conjugated core, and abundant edge-functional groups. The UCNSs show a novel tri-functional reinforcing effect for electrospinning carbon nanofiber fabrics including 32.6 times improved mechanical strength, 1.5 times improved electrical conductivity, as well as abundant edge oxygen-bearing functional groups as the pseudocapacitive active sites. Therefore, the free-standing fabric show high capacitive energy storage ability, good rate performance (191.2 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, 113.5 F g−1 at 50 A g−1), and outstanding cycle stability. The newly prepared UCNSs show high application potential, in-turn, provides a guidance for the high-efficient and high-end utilization of coal resources.

Xuemeng Gan: Experiment operation, Writing – original draft. Renlu Yuan: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Methodology. Jiayao Zhu: Experiment operation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Qiqi Li: Experiment operation, Methodology. Tingting Tang: Formal analysis. Fuwei Qin: Methodology. Linna Zhu: Formal analysis. Jun Zhang: Material characterization. Luxiang Wang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology. Su Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Draft revision, Supervision. Huaihe Song: Formal analysis, Methodology. Dianzeng Jia: Formal analysis, Methodology, Supervision, All authors contributed to the scientific discussion and manuscript revisions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52062046 and 51672021), the Science Foundation of Xinjiang Autonomous Region (2020D01C019), the Research Innovation Project of Postgraduate students in Xinjiang Autonomous Region (XJ2020G008), and the Xinjiang University Doctoral Research Foundation (XJUBSCX-201910).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.09.022.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Dong, S. Zhang, X. Du, S. Hong, S. Zhao, Y. Chen, X. Chen, H. Song, Boosting the electrical double-layer capacitance of graphene by self-doped defects through ball-milling. Adv. Funct. Mater. 29 (2019) 1901127.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Li, S. Zhang, H. Song, X. Chen, J. Zhou, S. Hong, New insight into the heteroatom-doped carbon as the electrode material for supercapacitors. Electrochim. Acta 180 (2015) 879-886.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Luo, Y. Chen, Y. Mo, A review of charge storage in porous carbon-based supercapacitors. New Carbon Mater. 36 (2021) 49-68.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Manipulation of nitrogen-heteroatom configuration for enhanced charge-storage performance and reliability of nanoporous carbon electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Sutarsis, C. Su, J. Li, D. Bresser, S. Passerini, J. Chang, Manipulation of nitrogen-heteroatom configuration for enhanced charge-storage performance and reliability of nanoporous carbon electrodes. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 12 (2020) 32797-32805.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancement of capacitance performance of flexible carbon nanofiber paper by adding graphene nanosheets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Tai, X. Yan, J. Lang, Q. Xue, Enhancement of capacitance performance of flexible carbon nanofiber paper by adding graphene nanosheets. J. Power Sources 199 (2012) 373-378.
